pr_title: Dropdown to change column type in table example app
body:
If I'm reading this right, it looks like we always allow the user to interact with the drop-down to change the type. But if the column has any cells, then we don't allow the update to go through.

It would probably be better to make it such that the UI disallows interacting with the drop-down when the column has any populated cells.

Note that in either case, due to collaboration, it will be possible for the type value to change while a column contains cells (e.g., one user makes the type change through the UI, and another user inserts a cell at the same time). So we'll also want some form of validation / handling of invalid cell content under a column. A "simple" way to do this might just be to highlight the cell in red if its content type doesn't match the column type.
- We don't need to add this handling in this PR, just noting that we'll need to tolerate cell data whose type disagrees with the column type (so we shouldn't throw an error or anything).

I generally recommend writing summary comments in terms of raw semantics as much as possible. E.g. "A node representing date and time information...". We probably want to link to documentation for JavaScript's `Date` type as well, since the underlying format we are persisting is based on that.

Also, to make the type a bit easier to use, you could add a static `fromDate` function that takes in a `Date` object and returns a new `DateTime` instance. That way users don't have reason about the `raw` field.

pr_title: add missing unit tests for tableSchema
body:
I asked Josh about this when I was reviewing this code, and the reason this was wrapped up in this function like this was to avoid running the code in createColumn, createRow and createTable during test enumeration.

Generally is nice to reduce what code runs during test enumeration so operations like running a single test are faster, but also since errors that happen during test enumeration are annoying to debug since they prevent all tests from running at all: consider a bug which causes a crash in createColumn: that would be real annoying to debug since you couldn't just see the tests for it fail, since no tests could run for the whole package.

There is a tradeoff though: generally its cleaner to put schema in static contexts, which run during test enumeration.

I think it's worth preserving the work Josh did to avoid running these schema creations during test enumeration.

Also making Cell, Column, Rot and Table schema at the top level in this file makes it annoying to test tables with different Cell, Row, Column or Table types (ex: if you wanted to test customized Rot subclasses with added methods or something like that)

these tests are referring to an invalid location for a cell as an invalid cell. As a reader here, I'd expect an invalid cell to be something that is not valid to use as a cell (ex: a node that does not comply with the Table's Cell schema).

Also "fails" is not well defined here. If we expect it to error, then is erring a success or a failure? Thus I don't think we should use "fails" in our phrasing.

I think it would be better to rephrase these tests' descriptions. For example this one could become "removing cell from nonexistent row and column errors"

body:
PR description says we are updating from 1.7.7 to 1.8.4, but before this change we had:

0.28.1, 1.7.9 (two copies)

Now we have:

0.28.1 (two copies), 1.7.9, 1.8.4 (two copies)


This doesn't match the PR description.

I suggest you:
1. Checkout latest main. Confirm `pnpm i --no-frozen-lockfile` on clean main doesn't make any changes. This ensures none of these changes are unrelated to your PR.
2. Merge latest main into this branch. (This ensure the above check actually applies to your PR)
3. Revert the changes to this lock file replacing it with the one from main. This ensure that experiments and such you did with earlier versions on this branch have no impact on the lock file.
4. Run `pnpm i --no-frozen-lockfile` on this branch so it should only do changes which correspond to the changes you have in the package.json files in this branch.

That will likely get you a smaller diff.

Then you can update the PR descript to seperatly cover both:
1. The changes to the package.json files for what versions of axios we support being used with our packages (You are dropping support for axios <= 1.8.4)
2. What versions of axios we install for development and testing

body:
## Pull Request Overview

This pull request updates the todo app to use the new TreeDataObject class and the associated tree-based data model. Key changes include updating entry points and file extensions (e.g. switching from index.ts to index.tsx), replacing the old todo model with the new tree-based model, and refactoring related UI components to work with the new tree API.

### Reviewed Changes

Copilot reviewed 10 out of 11 changed files in this pull request and generated 2 comments.

<details>
<summary>Show a summary per file</summary>

| File | Description |
| ---- | ----------- |
| examples/data-objects/todo/webpack.config.cjs | Updated the entry file reference to use the new index.tsx file. |
| examples/data-objects/todo/src/index.tsx | Introduced a new application entry point using the tree-based todo model. |
| examples/data-objects/todo/src/index.ts | Removed the legacy todo model implementation. |
| examples/data-objects/todo/src/TodoItem/* | Adjusted TodoItem exports and view to work with tree-based properties. |
| examples/data-objects/todo/src/Todo/schema.ts | Added a new schema for the TreeDataObject and related classes. |
| examples/data-objects/todo/src/Todo/TodoTreeView.tsx | Refactored the view to use tree-react-API and tree events. |
| examples/data-objects/todo/src/Todo/TodoTree.tsx | Implemented the new TreeDataObject data model for the todo list. |
| examples/data-objects/todo/src/Todo/Todo.ts | Removed the legacy todo implementation. |
</details>


<details>
<summary>Files not reviewed (1)</summary>

* **examples/data-objects/todo/package.json**: Language not supported
</details>

A few naming suggestions:

- `TodoTreeDataObject` => `TodoDataObject`

body: That's because `asFuzzView` does not return anything. It asserts that something is true after it has executed (maybe a better name would be `convertToFuzzView` or `fuzzify`, lol) - and then TS can count on that assertion in its type checking thereafter. If you prefer the return-style function instead, that's fine. But, think about how your function will behave if somebody adds a new property to `FuzzView` in the future. If that happens, you want your function to stop compiling, so that they know they need to go fix it. If that is currently the case, great! If not, the types need to better express what's actually going on.

`super({ ...options, jsonValidator: options.jsonValidator ?? typeboxValidator })`

In either case, you can change the default value for the `options` parameter to be `{}`, and you should follow the same pattern in the other constructor of your new subclass (because _it_ could be subclassed).

body:
Having to pass multiple things through a bunch of functions like this can be kindof annoying, and when one (or more) of them are option, its really fragile, since if any of the code paths forget to pass the optional one down, the code still builds and mostly works.

A design pattern for cases like this that is more robust is to group all the things you need to pass to all these places into a single object, and pass that around.

Before you change, the ChunkPolicy was filling this roll.

THis suggests two approaches that might work well here:
1. Make a new object that holds both the chunk policy and the new data (either as two separate fields of an aggregator object, or as a single object with both sets of data like `{...chunkPolicy, idCompressor}`
2. extend ChunkPolicy so that it has all the needed data.

I think either of these approaches would be fine here, but maybe its useful to be able to have a distinct type for a policy that does not have a compressor (ex: for things like defaultChunkPolicy), some maybe the fiirst option, and creating a new type, maybe we call it  ChunkCompressor thats `{policy: ChumPolicy, idCompressor: IIdCompressor | undefined }`

I don't think this is the ideal place to apply this logic. I think it would make more sense to do it in uniformChunkFromCursor. Specifically it (and also `insertValues` which it calls) should take in an optional idCompressor, document that string values will be compressed when possible if its provided.

This approach would avoid a second pass through the data, as well as avoid constructing the uniform chunk having to modify or copy its data array.

This would also make it possible in the future when we have optimizations for fast access to compressed identifiers for uniformChunkFromCursor to not decompress then recompress identifiers.

Also more generally, uniformChunkFromCursor will eventually get optimizations (like the above one, but also others) to allow it to process already compressed data without having to traverse it again, so integrating the logic in there will be necessary once we try and get that working efficiently and make 0 passes through the data in that special case.

I don't think that either of these "unhydrated" tests are actually testing anything interesting. Once the nodes are hydrated, they're hydrated. Whether they used to be unhydrated, or never existed in an unhydrated state, doesn't matter. 

The interesting test would be to create an iterator for an unhydrated node, _then_ hydrate it, and then check that the iterator keeps working. I expect that this will not work with the `hydrate` function, because our `hydrate` function doesn't actually hydrate unhydrated nodes :) I have a task to fix this but I've been unsuccessful so far.
